title: Functions & Quadratics
blurb: Vertices, roots, discriminants, parameters — the parabola in every disguise it wears on exams.

forms.title: Reading the vertex
forms.skill: y = (x−h)² + k has vertex (h; k) — mind the sign of h.

variants.statement: The parabola $y = (x - 3)^2 - 4$ has its vertex at…
variants.explanation: Vertex form $y = (x - h)^2 + k$ puts the vertex at $(h; k)$: here $(3; -4)$. Watch the sign: $(x - h)$ means the vertex sits at $+h$.

variants.statement: The equation $(x + 5)(x + 4) = 0$ has roots $x_1$ and $x_2$. Find $x_1 + x_2$.
variants.explanation: A product is zero when a factor is zero: $x = -5$ or $x = -4$. Sum: $-9$. (Vieta agrees: for $x^2 + 9x + 20$, the root sum is $-9$.)

forms.title: Composing functions
forms.skill: f(g(x)): evaluate the inner function first.

variants.statement: If $f(x) = 2x - 3$ and $g(x) = x^2$, find $f(g(3))$.
variants.explanation: Inside first: $g(3) = 9$. Then $f(9) = 2 \cdot 9 - 3 = 15$. Composing in the wrong order gives $(2\cdot3 - 3)^2$ — order matters.

variants.statement: If $f(x) = 2x - 3$ and $g(x) = x^2$, find $f(g(4))$.
variants.explanation: Inside first: $g(4) = 16$. Then $f(16) = 2 \cdot 16 - 3 = 29$. Composing in the wrong order gives $(2\cdot4 - 3)^2$ — order matters.

variants.statement: If $f(x) = 2x - 3$ and $g(x) = x^2$, find $f(g(5))$.
variants.explanation: Inside first: $g(5) = 25$. Then $f(25) = 2 \cdot 25 - 3 = 47$. Composing in the wrong order gives $(2\cdot5 - 3)^2$ — order matters.

variants.statement: If $f(x) = 2x - 1$ and $g(x) = x^2$, find $f(g(2))$.
variants.explanation: Inside first: $g(2) = 4$. Then $f(4) = 2 \cdot 4 - 1 = 7$. Composing in the wrong order gives $(2\cdot2 - 1)^2$ — order matters.

forms.title: Counting roots with the discriminant
forms.skill: D = b² − 4ac: positive two, zero one, negative none.

variants.statement: How many real roots does $x^2 + 5x + 6 = 0$ have?
variants.explanation: Discriminant: $D = (5)^2 - 4 \cdot 1 \cdot (6) = 1$. Positive ⟹ two distinct roots.

forms.title: Minimum via completing the square
forms.skill: x² + bx + c = (x + b/2)² + (c − b²/4); the constant IS the minimum.

variants.statement: Find the MINIMUM value of $y = x^2 + 4x - 6$.
variants.explanation: Complete the square: $y = (x + 2)^2 + (-10)$. A square is never negative, so the minimum is $-10$, reached at $x = -2$.

forms.title: Quadratic inequalities
forms.skill: Factor, find roots, read the sign between/outside.

variants.explanation: Factor: $(x + 5)(x + 3) < 0$. An upward parabola is NEGATIVE between its roots: $-5 < x < -3$.
Factor: $(x + 5)(x + 2) < 0$. An upward parabola is NEGATIVE between its roots: $-5 < x < -2$.
Factor: $(x + 5)(x + 1) < 0$. An upward parabola is NEGATIVE between its roots: $-5 < x < -1$.

Factor: $(x + 4)(x + 1) < 0$. An upward parabola is NEGATIVE between its roots: $-4 < x < -1$.

forms.title: Parameter for exactly one root
forms.skill: One root ⟺ D = 0; solve for the parameter.

variants.statement: For which $m$ does $x^2 - 4x + m = 0$ have exactly ONE root?
variants.explanation: Exactly one root needs $D = 0$: $(-4)^2 - 4m = 0 \Rightarrow m = \frac{16}{4} = 4$. Then $x^2 - 4x + 4 = (x - 2)^2$ — a perfect square kissing the axis.
